one_line_takeaway: Fine-tuned small LLMs top vulnerability-detection benchmarks only inside their training distribution and generalize poorly, and the benchmarks themselves are so mislabeled (per a Semgrep audit) that reported scores are hard to trust.
key_finding: Fine-tuning enables small LLMs to dominate in-distribution but destroys generalization. CodeLlama-7b-fine-tuned reaches ~97% F1 on the authors' own test set (97.13 vulnerable-class F1, 97.18 non-vulnerable-class F1) yet collapses out-of-distribution (e.g., non-vulnerable-class F1 of 7.76 on LineVul and 1.03 on Lin2017). Base LLMs show unstable, mostly poor cross-dataset F1 (typically ~30-50% averaged over both classes), with larger models being more stable but not strong. Separately, the Semgrep audit shows benchmark labels are unreliable: Semgrep flagged only 75 of 1,353 labeled-vulnerable functions in the authors' dataset and 0 of 7,054 in Choi2017, while flagging code labeled non-vulnerable as vulnerable (e.g., 161 such PrimeVul functions), casting doubt on both training and evaluation validity.
core_contribution: An empirical, cross-dataset benchmark study of how well LLMs detect vulnerabilities in source code, framed as binary classification (vulnerable vs. non-vulnerable). The authors' own contributions are: (1) a unified evaluation pipeline that runs six open-source models already trained for vulnerability detection (five VulBERTa-MLP variants + CodeBERT_finetuned_MSR) against six general-purpose LLMs (CodeBERT-base, Mistral-7b, Mixtral-8x7b, CodeLlama-7b, CodeLlama-13b, GPT-4-base), with three of the general models additionally fine-tuned by the authors; (2) a newly compiled, balanced 13,532-function C dataset; and (3) a dataset-quality audit using the industry SAST tool Semgrep that surfaces pervasive mislabeling in widely used benchmarks. The headline finding the authors claim as their own is that fine-tuning lets small LLMs beat much larger models, but only inside the exact distribution they were fine-tuned on, and that benchmark label quality is a first-order problem.
threat_model: This is a defensive-evaluation paper, not an adversarial-threat-model paper. There is no attacker, no attack surface, and no proposed defense against a specific adversary. The security context is pre-deployment detection of vulnerable code before it reaches production. The paper does touch a security-of-ML angle only in passing: it notes that mislabeled training datasets could corrupt fine-tuned models 'as in, e.g., poisoning attacks', but it does not study poisoning as a threat. No threat model is claimed and none should be inferred.
limitations: Task is reduced to coarse binary function-level classification (no CWE typing, no localization). The 'averaged F1 over both classes' the authors use to summarize performance is an informal indicator on imbalanced datasets and can obscure per-class collapse. GPT-4 is evaluated on only two datasets (budget), so its cross-dataset behavior is not comparable to the others. The Semgrep audit is itself limited: Semgrep operates on code snippets without project context/dependencies and reported large numbers of partially-scanned files (e.g., 10,000 issues on Choi2017, 11,975 on PrimeVul), so low Semgrep detection may partly reflect Semgrep's own snippet limitations rather than pure mislabeling. The authors' own dataset draws non-vulnerable samples from DiverseVul, and labels are assumed correct despite the paper's own thesis that labels are often wrong.

datasets_used: Authors' own dataset: 13,532 balanced C functions (6,766 vulnerable from GitHub CVEs/NVD 2002-2023, 6,766 non-vulnerable sampled from DiverseVul), 80/20 fine-tune/test split
Devign (27,318 functions: 12,460 vulnerable / 14,858 non-vulnerable) - test only
PrimeVul (25,908: 695 vulnerable / 25,213 non-vulnerable; official test split only) - test only
DiverseVul (source of the non-vulnerable half of the authors' dataset)
quantitative_results: CodeLlama-7b-fine-tuned on authors' own dataset: 97.13% F1 (vulnerable class) / 97.18% F1 (non-vulnerable class) - best in-distribution result
CodeLlama-7b-fine-tuned out-of-distribution collapse: non-vulnerable-class F1 = 7.76 on LineVul, 1.03 on Lin2017
Mistral-7b-fine-tuned on authors' dataset: 87.27% / 87.67% F1 (vulnerable / non-vulnerable); CodeBERT-fine-tuned: 72.50 / 70.54
GPT-4-base on authors' dataset: 70.51% (vulnerable-class F1) / 38.29% (non-vulnerable-class F1); on Lin2017: 15.88 / 32.27
Base/general LLMs cross-dataset F1 mostly ~30-50% averaged over both classes; best Choi2017 models (Mixtral-8x7b-base, Mistral-7b-fine-tuned) only ~47% combined
Semgrep audit (Table 4): detected only 75/1,353 labeled-vulnerable functions in authors' dataset, 0/7,054 in Choi2017, 104/1,055 in LineVul, 41/695 in PrimeVul, 26/44 in Lin2017, 166/12,460 in Devign
Semgrep flagged code labeled non-vulnerable as vulnerable: 161 (PrimeVul), 169 (Devign), 3 (authors' dataset)
baselines_compared: Task-specific vuln-detection models: VulBERTa-MLP-ReVeal, VulBERTa-MLP-D2A, VulBERTa-MLP-Draper, VulBERTa-MLP-MVD, VulBERTa-MLP-VulDeePecker, CodeBERT_finetuned_MSR
General-purpose base LLMs: CodeBERT-base, Mistral-7b-base, Mixtral-8x7b-base, CodeLlama-7b-base, CodeLlama-13b-base, GPT-4-base (gpt-4-0613)
Authors' fine-tuned models: CodeBERT-fine-tuned, Mistral-7b-fine-tuned, CodeLlama-7b-fine-tuned
Semgrep (industry SAST tool) used as an external label-quality reference (Snyk/SonarQube considered but unusable on isolated code snippets)
